content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ and you have to perform the following operation exactly XXX times:


Choose two integers iii and jjj such that 1≤i<j≤N1 \le i \lt j \le N1≤i<j≤N.
Choose a non-negative integer ppp.
Change AiA_iAi​ to Ai⊕2pA_i \oplus 2^pAi​⊕2p, and change AjA_jAj​ to Aj⊕2pA_j \oplus 2^pAj​⊕2p, where ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes bitwise XOR.
Find the lexicographically smallest sequence which can be obtained by performing this operation exactly XXX times.

A sequence B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​ is said to be lexicographically smaller than a sequence C1,C2,…,CNC_1, C_2, \ldots, C_NC1​,C2​,…,CN​ if there is an index iii such that Bi<CiB_i \lt C_iBi​<Ci​ and for each valid j<ij \lt ij<i, Bj=CjB_j = C_jBj​=Cj​.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and X. The second line contains N space-separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space-separated integers – the lexicographically smallest sequence that can be obtained after performing exactly X operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The original sequence is (2,2,3). Consider the following three operations: Choose i=1, j=2 and p=1. Then, A1 changes from 2 to 2⊕21=0 and similarly, A2 changes from 2 to 2⊕21=0. Now the sequence is (0,0,3). Next, choose i=1, j=2 and p=1. Then, A1 changes to 0⊕21=2 and A2 changes to 0⊕21=2. The sequence is (2,2,3). Next, again choose i=1, j=2 and p=1. Then, A1 changes to 2⊕21=0 and A2 changes to 2⊕21=0. The sequence is (0,0,3) again.
No operations are needed as the sequence is already lexicographically smallest.
Perform the operations on the smallest possible indices first to get the lexicographically smallest sequence.

solution.approach: The key insight is to realize that the lexicographically smallest sequence can be obtained by performing the operations on the smallest possible indices first. We can achieve this by iterating through the array from left to right and performing the operations on the smallest possible indices. The algorithm used is a simple iterative approach that keeps track of the number of operations performed and the current state of the array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * X) per test case, O(T * N * X) total. No precomputation is done.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the array of size N.
